{"date": "2026-07-12T00:15:54.506780+00:00", "transcription": "[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
RQ3, session A, TV1.
[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
When the sunlight strikes raindrops in the air, they act as a prism and form a rainbow.
[TV dialogue - redacted (copyrighted broadcast content); structure preserved]
The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colors. These take the shape of a long, round arch with its path high above and its two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot of gold at one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he is looking for the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow.
Throughout the centuries, people have explained the rainbow in various ways. Some have accepted it as a miracle without physical explanation. To the Hebrews, it was a token that there would be no more universal floods. The Greeks used to imagine that it was a sign from the gods to foretell war or heavy rain.
The Norsemen considered the rainbow as a bridge over which the gods passed from the earth to their home in the sky.
